edge: The abstract mentions that image generation models often generate incorrect associations between entities and their visual attributes.
The abstract highlights the impaired mapping between linguistic binding of entities and modifiers in the prompt and the visual binding of the corresponding elements in the generated image.
The abstract discusses the impaired mapping between the linguistic binding of entities and modifiers in the prompt and the visual binding of the corresponding elements in the generated image.
The abstract talks about the need for a novel loss function that encourages cross-attention maps to agree with the linguistic binding reflected by the syntax involving entities and their modifiers.
The proposal of SynGen aims to encourage large overlap between attention maps of entities and their modifiers and small overlap with other entities and modifier words.
The proposed approach emphasizes the large overlap between attention maps of entities and their modifiers to improve the faithfulness of text-to-image generation.
The proposed approach encourages small overlap between attention maps of entities and modifiers with other entities and modifier words.
The human evaluation demonstrates significant improvements of SynGen compared with current state-of-the-art methods in text-to-image generation.
The abstract highlights how the use of sentence structure during inference can efficiently and substantially improve the faithfulness of text-to-image generation.
Users control the generated content through natural-language text prompts.
Users control the generated content through natural-language text prompts.
Unfortunately, in many cases the generated images are not faithful to the text prompt.
Visual bindings of objects and their attributes may fail to match the linguistic bindings between entities and their modifiers.
In many cases, the generated images are not faithful to the text prompt due to improper binding where modifier words fail to influence the visual attributes of the entity-nouns.
SynGen corrects errors by matching the cross-attention maps of entities and their modifiers.
Current models often confuse the modifiers of the two entity-nouns in generating images.
The attribute may be completely neglected and missed from the generated image.

Images can be generated with objects that do not match their attributes due to text-based control. This highlights the importance of understanding the linguistic structure of the prompt for accurate image generation.

Diffusion models that use text encoders like CLIP may fail to encode linguistic structures, leading to a blind diffusion process. This affects the generation of objects that do not match their attributes.

Intervention in the generation process is proposed by steering the cross-attention maps of the diffusion model. These maps link prompt terms to image pixels, enhancing the visual binding between objects and their attributes.

Previous work aimed to improve relations between prompt terms and visual components, but did not specifically address the problem of modifier-entity binding. This issue is tackled by the proposed approach through a novel loss function.
The novel loss function quantifies the distance between attention patterns of grammatically-related pairs and pairs of unrelated words.
The attention patterns of grammatically-related pairs involve modifiers and entity-nouns.
The attention map separates modifiers from unrelated tokens and brings them closer to their grammatically-related nouns.
The latent denoised image improves pairing between attributes and objects by intervening in the latent code.
Intervening in the latent code improves the pairing between attributes and objects in the generated image.
The method is evaluated on three datasets.
The natural-language setting uses natural compositional prompts in the ABC-6K benchmark for evaluation.
Direct comparison is made with the previous state-of-the-art setting.
The set of prompts includes a variety of modifiers and entity-nouns for evaluation.
SynGen shows significant improvement in performance based on human evaluation.
The work highlights the effectiveness of incorporating linguistic information into text-conditioned image generation models.
Linguistic information is incorporated into text-conditioned image generation models to enhance their effectiveness.
Text prompt is analyzed to extract entity-nouns and their modifiers in the SynGen workflow and architecture.
SynGen adds intermediate steps to the diffusion denoising process by updating the latent representation using a loss over cross attention maps of entity-nouns and their modifiers.
The diffusion process is enriched with syntactic information in the paper's main contributions.
Inference-time optimization is used in the novel method to enrich the diffusion process with syntactic information.

In this work, we target the improper binding problem with our method.
Diffusion models face a common failure mode of text-conditioned diffusion where objects and their attributes incorrectly correspond to the entity-nouns and their modifiers in the prompt.
We propose SynGen as an inference-time intervention to address the improper binding problem.
The loss function encourages syntax-related modifiers and entity-nouns to have overlapping cross-attention maps.
The loss function discourages an overlap from cross-attention maps of other words in the prompt.
Our method is challenged with three datasets, including DVMP.

DVMP is a new dataset that is specially-designed to draw out hard cases of improper-binding problem.
The method demonstrates improvement of over 100% across all three datasets over the previous state-of-the-art.
Our work highlights the importance of linguistic structure during denoising for attaining faithful text-to-image generation.
Our work suggests promising avenues for future research.
